title: Spot Phishing Messages
summary: Recognize fake messages designed to steal your information.
learnerPromise: Recognize fake messages designed to steal your information.
whyItMatters: Scammers send fake emails and texts that look real to trick you into giving up passwords or money. Learning the warning signs helps you avoid falling for them.
realLifeUses: Checking an email that claims to be from your bank.
Reviewing a text message about a package you were not expecting.
Deciding whether a link in a message is safe to click.
Warning a friend or family member about a suspicious message they received.
youWillLearn: Identify common signs of a phishing message.
Check the sender's address for small spelling tricks.
Know what to do before clicking a link.
Report or delete a suspicious message safely.
miniChallenge: Look at your email or texts and find one message that looks suspicious. Check the sender.
steps: Open your email or messaging app and look at recent messages.
Read the sender's address carefully — not just the display name.
Look for urgent language like 'Act now' or 'Your account will be closed'.
Hover over or long-press a link to see the real web address before clicking.
If something feels off, delete the message or report it without responding.
completionCriteria: You found at least one warning sign in a real or example message.
You can explain why you did or did not trust the message.
commonProblems: The message looks very real — scammers copy logos and colors from real companies.
You are not sure about a link — when in doubt, open a browser and go to the site directly.
A message claims to be from someone you know — contact that person another way to check.
tips: Real companies rarely ask for your password or card number by email.
Spelling mistakes and strange grammar are common in scam messages.
If a message creates panic or pressure, slow down and think before acting.
